The Author Behind the Books

About Kat Falls

Quick Facts

Writes
Science fiction thrillers for young readers

Best known for
Dark Life, Rip Tide, Inhuman, and Undaunted

Grew up in
Silver Spring, Maryland

Lives in
Evanston, Illinois

Education
BS in Communications - Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute
MFA in Screenwriting - Northwestern University

Teaches
Creative writing workshops, schools, libraries, and conferences

Family
Robert Falls, two dogs, a very old cat, and three now-grown children

Biography

Novelist Kat Falls writes science fiction thrillers for young people.

In Dark Life and its sequel, Rip Tide, Kat takes middle grade readers on an action-packed adventure under the sea. She describes them as "Underwater Westerns." Both books were designated as Junior Library Guild Selections. The Dark Life series has been translated into 18 languages, was nominated for children's literature awards in ten states, and remains in development for film at Disney.

Kat received the ABC New Voices pick for outstanding debut of 2010, a Best Books selection from Bank Street College of Education, and the Juvenile Literary Award from Friends of American Writers for Dark Life. She also made a guest appearance on the Today Show when Al Roker featured Dark Life on "Al's Book Club for Kids."

Her young adult novel, Inhuman, is a dystopian romance set after the outbreak of a mutagenic virus. Inhuman received glowing reviews from Publishers Weekly, School Library Journal, and VOYA, earned a starred review from Kirkus, and made the Tome It List for clean teen reads. The sequel, Undaunted, came out in March 2019.

Kat loves to share her joy of books and writing with audiences of all ages. She teaches creative writing workshops and speaks at schools, libraries, and writers' conferences around the country.

Kat grew up in Silver Spring, Maryland, holds a BS in communications from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute and an MFA in screenwriting from Northwestern University, where she now teaches. She lives in Evanston, Illinois with her husband, theater director Robert Falls, two dogs, and a very old cat. Her three children are now young adults, though still very influential in her creative process.

Awards and Recognition

  • * ABC New Voices pick for outstanding debut of 2010 - Dark Life
  • * Best Books selection, Bank Street College of Education - Dark Life
  • * Juvenile Literary Award, Friends of American Writers - Dark Life
  • * Junior Library Guild Selection - Dark Life and Rip Tide
  • * Al Roker's Book Club for Kids feature on the Today Show - Dark Life
  • * Translated into 18 languages - Dark Life series
  • * Nominated for children's literature awards in ten states - Dark Life series
  • * Kirkus starred review - Inhuman
  • * Tome It List for clean teen reads - Inhuman

More on Kat

Chicago Tribune, January 2014

By Courtney Crowder

Kat Falls, originally Moynihan, grew up in Silver Spring, Maryland in a family shaped by science, research, and curiosity. She wrote constantly as a child and knew early that she wanted a creative life, eventually studying communications at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute before moving into media studies and screenwriting.

After time in New York and graduate work at Northwestern, Kat built the foundation for the science-rich fiction she would later become known for. She has described how her family's scientific background, her own coursework, her taste for speculative fiction, and her love of research all fed directly into the worlds she creates on the page.

The original spark for Dark Life came from wanting to write the kind of high-tension, immediately gripping story her son would not put down. To make that world feel real, Kat researched deep-sea architecture, subsea living, and emerging technologies such as oxygen-rich fluorocarbon liquids for divers.

By the time Rip Tide followed, Kat had already established herself as a writer who could blend plausible science with commercial adventure. The article also notes Disney and the Gotham Group developing Dark Life for film and highlights how quickly Kat moved from debut novelist to a writer building a lasting body of work for young readers.

Teaching

Workshops and Speaking

Kat shares her joy of books and writing with audiences of all ages. She teaches creative writing workshops and speaks at schools, libraries, and writers' conferences around the country.

Her background in screenwriting, publishing, and science-informed fiction makes her especially well suited for conversations about craft, research, storytelling momentum, and writing for young readers.

Northwestern

Teacher and Mentor

Kat earned her MFA in screenwriting from Northwestern University and now teaches there. That full-circle path shows up throughout her work: she is both a practicing novelist and a teacher who helps other writers sharpen voice, pacing, and emotional stakes.

Northwestern Faculty Page ->

FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

These are some of the questions Kat is often asked. If yours is not answered here yet, reach out and it can be added in a future update.

Where did you get the idea for Dark Life?

I came up with the idea during a writing exercise. My oldest son was 11 at the time, and I decided to try combining three things that he loved to read about into one story: the ocean, Old West pioneers, and the X-men. Suddenly, the world of the story took shape in my mind and the plot came together fairly easily after that.

Did you have to do any research for Dark Life?

So much so, I had to cut myself off on a regular basis. I would get on the internet to find the answer to one small question and two hours would zip by without my noticing. There is just so much to learn about the ocean.

I read up on everything from marine life and subsea geography to future technology. Every creature mentioned in Dark Life and Rip Tide is real and can be found in the ocean today.

Are any of the technologies, like Liquigen or the underwater architecture, possible in reality?

Because I wanted to write science fiction and not fantasy, I did not want to stray too far from the possible. Therefore I tried to base all the technology in the story on some prediction or theory posed by an engineer, scientist, or architect.

In some cases, the science has been developed but is still in the experimental stages. For example, liquid breathing, which was my inspiration for Liquigen, is currently used to help premature babies and coma patients who are in respiratory distress. However, it is a long way from being something people use for scuba diving.

Would you describe Dark Life as a dystopian novel?

The Topside is definitely dystopian with its overcrowded stack-cities, but the story really takes place out on the ocean frontier, which is still a wilderness. Certainly the word "dystopian" never entered my mind while I was writing.

The two genres I intentionally mixed were science fiction and the western. So I have pioneers and outlaws, but the story is set in the future. It is like a space-western... underwater. I had a lot of fun with the western cliches and tropes, trying to find their subsea equivalents.

One of the themes I explore in both books is that the survival of a group depends on the individuals' willingness to accept one another and work together. It is a theme straight out of one of my favorite westerns, Stagecoach, and an idea that still feels relevant today on a global scale.

Why did you decide to have Ty narrate the story, rather than Gemma?

I did briefly consider having Gemma narrate the book because as a writer, it is much easier to describe people and places if a character is new to the environment. But after trying it in her point of view, I realized quickly that Gemma's reactions to the subsea world were similar to ours and that her thoughts were not surprising.

Getting inside Ty's head, however, forced me to think like someone who has spent his whole life underwater. The things he does not say, his feelings and reactions, often surprised me as I was writing and that is always fun.

Are your characters inspired by people you know?

Travis, the 14 year-old protagonist of Old Yeller, served as my inspiration for Ty. Travis hunts with a rifle to keep his mother and little brother fed and must work the family farm when his father leaves on a cattle drive. I wanted the same to be true for Ty, though his family's homestead is on the ocean floor and he hunts for dinner with a harpoon gun.

Ty's little sister, Zoe, was based somewhat on my daughter who was eight when I wrote Dark Life. The way she interacts with Ty is a lot like what goes on in my house every day.

Gemma is based on a character type you find in western films: the easterner who is a fish out of water in the old west. She is Ty's opposite in many ways, but they share the same core qualities. They are both brave, loyal, and willing to risk everything for the people they love.

Does Dark Life have a book trailer?

It has two. The US version is available at bcove.me/dcwboxhq.

The UK version is at youtube.com/watch?v=8iiQSwmd_gs.

Will there be a sequel?

Yes, it is called Rip Tide and it came out in August 2011.

What is your favorite sea creature?

I have always loved whales, but after all my research I also fell hard for sea slugs. They are unbelievably beautiful little creatures and so varied. Go punch "nudibranch" into your web browser and you will see what I mean.

Topsider or subsea pioneer? What would you be and why?

As much as I would love to visit the homesteads of Benthic Territory, I do not think I would be very good at farming kelp and seaweed. But I know I would be miserable living in a stack-city where there is no nature or wildlife, so I think I would be a floater: someone who lives on a houseboat on the ocean's surface.

Are your books in any other formats?

There is an audio book of Dark Life. Keith Nobbs is the narrator and he does a great job.

Did you always like to write?

For as long as I can remember, I have told myself stories, a habit that my teachers called daydreaming. I used to get in trouble for it. I would stare out the window, not paying attention to what was happening around me, and weave some exciting adventure in my mind. Actually, I still do that.

I started writing down my daydreams in elementary school and have kept a journal since fifth grade. But I did not think about getting paid for writing fiction until I was in college. It was just something I did for my own amusement.

How and where do you write?

I have a desk in a spare room that I have turned into a home office. I try to write when my kids are at school, though when I am working on a first draft I usually end up writing late at night, after my inner critic has gone to sleep.

I also like to be surrounded by pictures when I am writing. It keeps me immersed in my story world. I make collages of images and prop them on an easel that faces my desk. I swap them out depending on which scene I am working on.

Why the ocean and not vampires, witches or werewolves?

I love supernatural stories, but I love science fiction just as much, and it seemed to me that there were lots of fantasy books written for middle graders but very few sci-fi books.

Sci-fi and fantasy are often lumped together because they both take the reader into new worlds. The difference is that fantasy relies on elements impossible in our world, while science fiction is based on the possible: things that might not exist right now, but could be invented someday or result from future innovation.

Do you have any advice for writers looking to get published?

Know your audience. As obvious as that sounds, knowing that I was writing for tweens helped me every time I was faced with a creative choice.

I had seen how ruthless my children could be about books, especially my oldest son. If a story did not grab him on page one, he would toss it aside and never give it another try. So I wrote Dark Life and Rip Tide with kids like him in mind. I kept my prose lean and loaded on the action and imagery to make it feel like a thrill ride.

Is Kat Falls your real name?

My full name is Kathleen Moynihan Falls. Moynihan is my maiden name. Since my friends call me Kat, it just made sense to use it as my author name. Plus, it is easy for readers to remember.

Kat Falls

NYT bestselling author of Dark Life and Inhuman.
Evanston, IL.
